paper_title: Inferred from content: Lipid Biomarker Analysis

methods.method_name: Extraction and Analysis of Lipid Biomarkers from Sediment
methods.description: Procedure for extracting, separating, identifying, and quantifying specific lipid biomarkers, including highly branched isoprenoids (HBIs) and sterols, from freeze-dried and homogenized sediment samples. The method involves solvent extraction with internal standards, separation by open silica gel column chromatography, silylation of the sterol fraction, analysis by gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC-MS), component identification based on retention times and mass spectra, quantification using internal standards and external calibration, normalization against sediment amount and TOC content, and calculation of PIP25 indices and accumulation rates.

steps.description: 5 g of freeze-dried and homogenized sediment were extracted in intervals between 1 and 3 cm.
steps.searchable_summary: Prepare freeze-dried and homogenized sediment samples for extraction.

steps.description: For the extraction, dichloromethane/methanol (DCM/MeOH, 2:1 v/v) was added to the sediment samples additionally with the internal standards 7-hexylnonadecane (7-HND, 0.076 mg/sample), 9-octylheptadec-8-ene (9-OHD, 0.1 mg/sample), 5a-androstan-3b-ol (androstanol, 10.7 mg/sample) and squalane (3.2 mg/sample) for the quantification of lipid biomarkers in each sample, followed by ultrasonification (15 min) and centrifugation (3 min) three times.
steps.searchable_summary: Perform solvent extraction of lipids from sediment using DCM/MeOH with internal standards, ultrasonification, and centrifugation.

steps.description: Subsequently, we separated the hydrocarbon fraction (5 ml n-hexane) from the sterol fraction (9 ml ethylacetate/n-hexane) by open silica gel column chromatography.
steps.searchable_summary: Separate hydrocarbon and sterol fractions from lipid extract using open silica gel column chromatography.

steps.description: Finally, the sterol fraction was silylated by adding 200 ml of BSTFA (bis-trimethylsilyl-trifluoroacet-amide) at 60 " C for 2 h.
steps.searchable_summary: Silylate the sterol fraction using BSTFA.

steps.description: The hydrocarbon fraction was measured with a gas chromatograph Agilent Technologies 7890B GC system (30m DB-1MS column, 0.25 mm i.d., 0.25 mm film thickness) coupled to a mass spectrometer Agilent 5977A MSD (70 eV constant ionization potential, Scan 50e550 m/z, 1 scan/s, ion source temperature 230 " C, Performance Turbo Pump) with the temperature program: 60 " C (3 min), 150 " C (heating rate: 15 " C/min), 320 " C (heating rate: 10 " C/min) and 320 " C (15 min, isothermal).
steps.searchable_summary: Analyze hydrocarbon fraction using GC-MS with specified parameters and temperature program.

steps.description: The sterols were measured with a GC Agilent 6850 GC coupled to an Agilent 5975C VL MSD (conditions see above) with the temperature sequence: 60 " C (2 min), then 150 " C (heating rate: 15 " C/min), 320 " C (heating rate: 3 " C/min) and 320 " C (20 min isothermal).
steps.searchable_summary: Analyze silylated sterol fraction using GC-MS with specified parameters and temperature program.

steps.description: Component assignment was based on comparison of GC retention times with those of reference compounds and published mass spectra (sterols: Boon et al., 1979;Volkman, 1986;HBIs: Belt et al., 2007;Brown and Belt, 2016).
steps.searchable_summary: Identify lipid biomarkers by comparing GC retention times and mass spectra to reference data.

steps.description: The concentration of each biomarker was calculated by setting its individual GC-MS ion responses in relation to those of respective internal standards. For the quantification of the sterols (quantified as trimethylsilyl ethers), the molecular ions m/z 470 for brassicasterol, m/z 472 for campesterol, m/z 486 for b-sitosterol, and m/z 500 for dinosterol were used in relation to the molecular ion m/z 348 of the internal standard androstanol. For the quantification of IP 25 , HBI II and HBI III, their molecular ions (m/z 350 for IP 25 , m/z 348 for HBI II, and m/z 346 for HBI III) were compared to the fragment ion m/z 266 of the internal standard 7-HND. The different responses of these ions were balanced by an external calibration.
steps.searchable_summary: Calculate biomarker concentrations using GC-MS ion responses relative to internal standards and external calibration.
inputs: Specific m/z values for biomarkers and standards

steps.description: All biomarkers of this study were normalized against the amount of sediment and TOC content.
steps.searchable_summary: Normalize biomarker concentrations by sediment amount and TOC content.

steps.description: We calculated the PIP 25 indices according to the equation by Müller et al. (2011), where c is a concentration balance factor (to account for concentration differences between IP 25 and phytoplankton biomarkers (P), i.e., brassicasterol, dinosterol or HBI III: [Equation not provided] On average, concentrations of brassicasterol are about five times higher, dinosterol about ten times higher and HBI III about two times lower than the IP 25 concentrations in Core PS93/025, resulting in balance factors of 0.23, 0.11 and 2.44, respectively.
steps.searchable_summary: Calculate PIP25 indices using the specified equation and concentration balance factors.
inputs: Normalized biomarker concentrations (IP25, brassicasterol, dinosterol, HBI III)

steps.description: Biomarker concentrations were converted into accumulation rates by using the following equations (e.g. Stein and Macdonald, 2004): [Equations not provided]
steps.searchable_summary: Convert biomarker concentrations into accumulation rates using specified equations.
inputs: Accumulation rate equations (from Stein and Macdonald, 2004 or similar)
Sedimentation rates or age model data (implied)
